title: CTEs (The WITH Clause) 🧩
description: Making complex subqueries infinitely more readable.

slides.title: Spaghetti Code 🍝
slides.content:
# The Problem with Subqueries

Subqueries are great, but if you have a subquery inside a subquery inside a subquery... your code becomes an unreadable mess of parentheses.

Enter **CTEs (Common Table Expressions)**. They let you define temporary tables clearly at the very top of your SQL query!

slides.title: The WITH Clause 🛠️
slides.content:
## Defining a CTE

You start a CTE using the `WITH` keyword.

```sql
WITH HighEarners AS (
  SELECT id, name FROM employees WHERE salary > 100000
)
SELECT name FROM HighEarners;
```

Think of `HighEarners` as a temporary variable that holds a mini-table. We can then `SELECT` from it as if it were a real table!

quizData.question: What is the primary benefit of using a CTE (Common Table Expression)?

options.text: It permanently saves a new table to the database physically
It makes complex queries much easier to read by defining temporary result sets at the top
It bypasses all security constraints automatically

quizData.explanation: CTEs are purely for readability and organization. They don't save anything permanently.

slides.title: Chaining CTEs 🔗
slides.content:
## Multiple Steps

You can define multiple CTEs by separating them with a comma.

```sql
WITH StepOne AS (
  SELECT * FROM users
),
StepTwo AS (
  SELECT * FROM StepOne WHERE active = true
)
SELECT * FROM StepTwo;
```

This makes logical step-by-step transformations incredibly easy to follow, top to bottom!

quizData.question: If you want to declare a second CTE right after the first one, how do you separate them syntax-wise?

options.text: By typing the word WITH again
By using a comma (,) between them

quizData.explanation: You only use the word WITH once. Subsequent CTEs are separated by commas.
